title: Pfizer — Financials
html:
Pfizer Financial Architecture & Budget Intelligence

Governing Thought

The Pfizer account is a mid-size, high-growth engagement generating $302K in FY25 with a 90% growth target to $575K in FY26. Profitability is strong at 61%, but the account's financial architecture is fundamentally different from J&J: revenue is concentrated in a single TA (Vaccines) across a small number of brands, with no preferred vendor discount and no enterprise-level SOW structure. The $273K growth gap depends almost entirely on entering new brands via HCPP — making revenue diversification both the biggest opportunity and the biggest risk. Understanding where the dollars come from today, where they need to come from tomorrow, and how Pfizer's brand-independent budget structure shapes pricing decisions is essential for managing this account's growth trajectory.

Where to Find Financial Data

System What It Holds Access Update Cadence
Kimble Revenue, project-level financials, effort tracking, invoicing Internal ZoomRx (web app) Real-time as PMs update
Cluster Management (OneDrive) Revenue/Wins/Pipeline by cluster & account; utilization OneDrive, Base 1 file Weekly refresh
AO Plan Rich Durante's account plan with brand-level targets, contacts, strategy Shared with Vinoth Updated semi-annually (V2 Oct 2025)

How they connect: Kimble gives the ZoomRx-side truth on project revenue and effort. Cluster Management shows Pfizer's share of cluster revenue alongside other accounts. The AO Plan provides the forward-looking strategy and brand-level target breakdown that Kimble doesn't capture until projects are booked.

Account Financial Summary

Metric FY25 Actual FY26 Target Delta
Total Revenue $302,250 $575,000 +$272,750 (90% growth)
Profitability 61% 61% (target) Maintained
AO Rich Durante (Principal) -- --
AD Nick Fleischman (SM) -- --

Revenue by Brand

Brand FY25 Base FY26 Target Delta Work Type
Abrysvo (Maternal) $216,000 $225,000 +$9,000 PET: $150K, HCPP: $75K
Abrysvo (OA) $0 $75,000 +$75,000 PET: $75K
Prevnar (Adult) $0 $75,000 +$75,000 HCPP: $75K
Prevnar (Pediatric) $0 $0 -- --
Trumenba/Penbraya $86,250 $75,000 -$11,250 HCPP: $75K
Other (non-vaccine) $0 $125,000 +$125,000 HCPP: $125K (2 brands)
Total $302,250 $575,000 +$272,750

Revenue Concentration Analysis

Dimension FY25 FY26 Target
Vaccines share of revenue 100% 78% (if "other brands" delivers)
Abrysvo Maternal share 72% 39%
Top brand dependency Abrysvo Maternal = 72% of total Still dominant but declining
Number of active brands 2 (Abrysvo Maternal, Trumenba) 4-6 (adding Abrysvo OA, Prevnar Adult, 2 non-vaccine)

Key risk: FY25 revenue is 72% dependent on a single brand (Abrysvo Maternal). Losing or significantly reducing that workstream would be catastrophic. The FY26 plan diversifies this risk, but only if the new brand entries convert.

Revenue Bridge: $302K to $575K

The FY26 growth plan requires $273K in net new revenue. Here is the bridge with confidence assessments:

Source Revenue Confidence Notes
Abrysvo Maternal organic growth +$9K High Renewal of existing PET + HCPP
Abrysvo OA (PET) +$75K High PET already signed/active
Prevnar Adult (HCPP) +$75K High HCPP signed for FY26
Trumenba/Penbraya (HCPP renewal) -$11K Medium Slight decline from FY25 base
Other brands (2 HCPP wins) +$125K Low-Medium Requires entering 2 new brands with no current contracts
Total +$273K

The gap: The first three items (+$159K) are high-confidence. The "$125K other brands" bucket is the most uncertain element of the plan and requires winning 2 new HCPP engagements on brands where ZoomRx has no current revenue (Paxlovid, Nurtec, Xeljanz, or Vyndaquel).

Upside Beyond $575K Target

If core plan hits AND Rich lands additional opportunities:

  • Abrysvo Maternal ATU ($50-75K) — displacing Sermo
  • Trumenba Digital Tracking ($50K) — nearly closed
  • One PET pilot conversion ($75K) — Trumenba or Prevnar Adult

That's $175-200K in upside, pushing toward $750K+ and setting up a FY27 target above $700K.

Pricing Architecture

Pricing Context

Unlike J&J, Pfizer does not have a preferred vendor discount with ZoomRx. This means:

  • Professional fees are at standard rates
  • Profitability (61%) is higher than J&J's margin despite lower volume
  • There is no structural pricing constraint limiting margin

Observed Pricing by Service Type

Based on current and recent Pfizer projects:

Service Observed Price Range Notes
PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracking) $75K-$150K per brand Abrysvo Maternal PET at $150K; Abrysvo OA PET at $75K
HCP-Patient Conversations (HCPP) $50-75K per brand Standard HCPP pricing across vaccine brands
Digital Tracking ~$50K Trumenba discussion-stage
ATU $50-75K (estimated) Not yet active at Pfizer; estimate based on cross-account benchmarks
Message Labs (AI Pilot) [Populate from project data] AI Migraine Messaging Pilot with Aimee Renaud

Pricing Heuristics for Pfizer

  • HCPP is the entry-price service: At $50-75K, it fits within a marketing lead's discretionary budget — critical for a brand-by-brand entry model
  • PET is the scale-up play: At $75-150K, it represents the largest per-brand revenue opportunity
  • Bundle pricing may work within-brand: On Abrysvo Maternal (already running PET + HCPP + Digital Tracking), bundling ATU at a modest discount could make consolidation rational for Tim Douglas vs. keeping Sermo's standalone ATU
  • Cross-brand bundling will NOT work: Pfizer brands have independent budgets; bundling across brands has no buyer on the other side

Active SOW Structure

Project Brand Service Type Revenue Type SOW Structure
PFZ_ABRYSVO_Maternal_HCP-Pt Study Abrysvo Maternal HCPP Recurring Per-study SOW
PFZ_ABRYSVO_Maternal_Digital Tracking Abrysvo Maternal Digital Tracking Recurring Per-study SOW
PFZ_ABRYSVO_OA_HCP-Pt Study Abrysvo OA HCPP Project Per-study SOW
PFZ_PREVNAR_HCP-Pt 2025 Prevnar Adult HCPP Project Per-study SOW
PFZ_Meningococcal_HCP-Pt Study Trumenba/Penbraya HCPP Recurring Per-study SOW
PFZ_Meningococcal_ATU Trumenba/Penbraya ATU Project Per-study SOW
PFZ_LORBRENA_PET Lorbrena PET Project Per-study SOW
PFZ_ABRYSVO_Maternal_HCP-Pt Study (repeat) Abrysvo Maternal HCPP Recurring Per-study SOW
PFZ_Respiratory_Vaccine_Pharmacist Pulse Vaccines (cross) Qual/Pulse One-time Per-study SOW
PFZ_Message Labs (AI Migraine Pilot) Above-brand (Nurtec?) Message Testing Pilot Per-study SOW
PFZ_Pharms Quant Vaccines Quant Project Per-study SOW

Structural observation: All Pfizer SOWs are per-study, project-based. There are no annualized tracking SOWs (unlike J&J's annual SFEA, OCE, and MSL contracts). This means:

  • Revenue requires active re-selling each cycle
  • There is no "baseline recurring" revenue that auto-renews
  • Converting Pfizer to annual tracking SOWs (starting with PET) would dramatically improve revenue predictability

Pfizer's Market Research Budget Intelligence

What We Know

Data Point Source Confidence
Pfizer total MR spend Unknown --
Vaccine team MR budget Unknown --
PET incumbent spend (BI) Unknown — BI runs most Pfizer PETs --
HCPP incumbent spend (Verilogue) Unknown — Verilogue active on select brands --
ATU incumbent spend (Sermo) Unknown — Sermo runs Abrysvo Maternal ATU --

Critical gap: Unlike J&J (where Heather Kulesik volunteered budget estimates), no Pfizer contact has shared MR budget intelligence. This limits ZoomRx's ability to size the total addressable opportunity or benchmark pricing.

Action items:

  • Ask Rich Durante whether AO plan contains any budget intelligence beyond the brand-level targets
  • During next client meeting, explore whether Tim Douglas or Antonietta Forte can share directional budget ranges for their brands
  • Research Pfizer annual report / investor presentations for MR budget signals

Pfizer Financial Context (Public Data)

Pfizer's overall financial trajectory provides context for MR spending decisions:

Metric 2025 Notes
Total Revenue ~$63.6B (Q4 guidance) Post-COVID normalization; growth driven by non-COVID portfolio
Abrysvo Revenue ~$1B 136% operational growth in Q4 2025; international expansion driving growth
Prevnar Family Revenue ~$6B+ annually 8% operational growth Q4 2025; 3 billionth dose milestone
Paxlovid Revenue Declining Post-pandemic normalization; commercial model shifting
Nurtec Revenue Growing Competitive migraine market (vs. AbbVie's Ubrelvy, Lilly's Emgality)
Oncology (ex-Seagen) Ibrance ~$4B+; Padcev/Adcetris growing Seagen acquisition expanding oncology portfolio significantly

Implication for MR budgets: Pfizer's overall MR investment likely correlates with brand lifecycle stage:

  • Growth brands (Abrysvo, Penbraya, Padcev): Higher MR spend, more open to new vendors
  • Mature brands (Prevnar, Ibrance, Eliquis): Established vendor relationships, harder to displace
  • Declining brands (Paxlovid, Xeljanz generic erosion): Budget pressure, but may seek fresh insights

Financial Watch Items

Revenue Concentration Risk (HIGH)

FY25 is 72% dependent on Abrysvo Maternal. Even in FY26, vaccines will remain ~78% of revenue. A single event — budget cut, re-org, competitor displacement, or CDC recommendation change — that impacts the Abrysvo franchise would significantly damage the account. The FY25 Abrysvo Adult PET loss (mentioned in SWOT) is a precedent: it happened before.

Mitigation: Execute the brand diversification plan aggressively. Every non-vaccine brand win reduces this concentration risk.

The "$125K Other Brands" Uncertainty (HIGH)

This is the weakest part of the FY26 plan. It requires 2 new HCPP wins on brands where ZoomRx has zero current revenue and, in most cases, no established contact. The AO plan's Q1 milestone (use HCPP vax engagements as use cases) and Q2 milestone (expand into PET with zero-cost pilots) are sequential — the pipeline depends on Q1 delivery quality creating advocacy.

Mitigation: Prioritize Paxlovid (Gavin Hansen is warm) and Nurtec (via Message Labs / Aimee Renaud connection). These are the two most viable "other brand" candidates.

No Annual Tracking SOWs (MEDIUM)

All Pfizer revenue is project-based with no annual contracts. This means revenue is inherently lumpy and requires active selling each quarter. Converting even one PET to an annual tracking SOW would provide baseline recurring revenue.

Mitigation: When the Trumenba or Prevnar Adult PET pilot converts, negotiate an annual tracking structure rather than per-wave pricing.

Seasonal Revenue Patterns (MEDIUM)

RSV vaccines (Abrysvo) have a concentrated vaccination window (August-January), which likely concentrates related research spending into Q2-Q4. Pneumococcal is less seasonal. Meningococcal peaks in back-to-school season. This means Pfizer vaccine research revenue may be front-loaded in H2 of each fiscal year.

Mitigation: Ensure non-vaccine brand wins (Paxlovid, Nurtec) are spread across the year to smooth revenue.

BI Displacement Economics (LOW but strategic)

Displacing BI from PETs would be the single largest revenue accelerator on the account. Each PET won represents $75-150K. However, zero-cost pilots (the AO plan's Q2 strategy) temporarily compress margins. Budget for 1-2 unpaid pilots in FY26 with the expectation of paid conversion in FY27.

Financial Questions to Answer

Pull these from Kimble and AO plan reviews:

Revenue & Pipeline

  • What is Pfizer's trailing-12-month revenue to ZoomRx (Kimble)?
  • What Pfizer SOWs are expiring in the next 90 days and need renewal?
  • What is the current pipeline (proposals submitted but not yet won)?
  • Has the Trumenba Digital Tracking converted to a signed SOW?

Profitability

  • What is the blended margin across Pfizer projects?
  • Are any projects running over-effort (Kimble hours vs. budget)?
  • What is the cost of zero-cost PET pilots to the cluster?

Cluster Context

  • What share of the cluster's total revenue comes from Pfizer?
  • How does Pfizer's win rate compare to other accounts in the cluster?
  • What is the effort allocation for Pfizer projects vs. other accounts?

[Populate from Kimble pull and Rich Durante / Nick Fleischman debrief.]

Back to Accounts Hub | Home
